---
title: "Why does delivery overpayment vary so much by utility?"
subtitle: "Decomposing the Bill Alignment Test across NY's seven electric utilities"
reference-location: margin
fig-cap-location: bottom
execute:
  warning: false
  echo: true
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| code-fold: false

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import subprocess

import polars as pl
import yaml
from great_tables import GT
from lib.rdp import fetch_rdp_file
from lib.plotnine import SB_COLORS, theme_switchbox
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    element_text,
    geom_col,
    geom_point,
    geom_text,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    position_dodge,
    scale_fill_manual,
    scale_x_continuous,
    scale_y_continuous,
    theme,
)

BATCH = "ny_20260307_r1-8_gascalcfix"
RDP_REF = "d0353e1"
S3_BASE = "s3://data.sb/switchbox/cairo/outputs/hp_rates"
PATH_BAT_12 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_1+2/cross_subsidization_BAT_values/"
HEATING_TYPE_COL = "postprocess_group.heating_type"

UTIL_DISPLAY: dict[str, str] = {
    "coned": "ConEd",
    "nimo": "NiMo",
    "nyseg": "NYSEG",
    "cenhud": "CenHud",
    "rge": "RG&E",
    "or": "O&R",
    "psegli": "PSEG-LI",
}


def _wmean(df: pl.DataFrame, col: str, w: str = "weight") -> float:
    return float((df[col] * df[w]).sum() / df[w].sum())


def _wmedian(df: pl.DataFrame, col: str, w: str, q: float = 0.5) -> float:
    s = df.sort(col)
    cum = s[w].cum_sum() / s[w].sum()
    return float(s.filter(cum >= q)[col][0])
```

## The finding

The NY HP Rates report shows that HP customers systematically overpay on delivery — the Bill Alignment Test (BAT)
is positive for nearly every HP household. But the *size* of the overpayment varies dramatically across utility
territories.

```{python}
#| label: load-bat

bat = pl.read_parquet(PATH_BAT_12, hive_partitioning=True)
hp = bat.filter(pl.col(HEATING_TYPE_COL) == "heat_pump")

UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT = (
    hp.group_by("sb.electric_utility")
    .agg(
        (pl.col("BAT_percustomer_delivery") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
        / pl.col("weight").sum()
    )
    .sort("BAT_percustomer_delivery", descending=True)["sb.electric_utility"]
    .to_list()
)

print(f"Total buildings: {bat.height:,}")
print(f"HP buildings: {hp.height:,}")
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: bat-summary-table

rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    sub = hp.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    rows.append({
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "N (buildings)": sub.height,
        "Mean BAT": round(_wmean(sub, "BAT_percustomer_delivery")),
        "Median BAT": round(_wmedian(sub, "BAT_percustomer_delivery", "weight")),
    })

summary = pl.DataFrame(rows)

(
    GT(summary)
    .fmt_number(columns=["N (buildings)"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Mean BAT", "Median BAT"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="Delivery BAT for HP customers by utility")
)
```
:::

CenHud's mean HP overpayment is \$1,737/yr while ConEd's is only \$556/yr — a **3x ratio**. What's driving this?

## Bills vs. cost of service: where the gap comes from

The BAT is simply the gap between what an HP customer *pays* (their delivery bill) and what they *cost* (their cost
of service). Cost of service has two pieces: the customer's **marginal cost** (their share of delivery infrastructure
costs, based on when and how much they consume) and a **residual share** (an equal per-customer allocation of the
remaining delivery revenue).

Let's visualize the bill and cost side by side for the mean HP customer in each utility.

```{python}
#| label: compute-bill-vs-cost

bill_cost_rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    sub = hp.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    bill = _wmean(sub, "annual_bill_delivery")
    mc = _wmean(sub, "economic_burden_delivery")
    resid = _wmean(sub, "residual_share_delivery")
    bat_val = _wmean(sub, "BAT_percustomer_delivery")
    cos = mc + resid
    bill_cost_rows.append({
        "util": util,
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "Marginal Cost": round(mc),
        "MC %": mc / cos if cos else 0,
        "Residual": round(resid),
        "Cost of Service": round(cos),
        "Delivery Bill": round(bill),
        "BAT": round(bat_val),
    })

bill_cost_df = pl.DataFrame(bill_cost_rows)
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: bill-cost-table

(
    GT(bill_cost_df.drop("util"))
    .fmt_currency(
        columns=["Marginal Cost", "Residual", "Cost of Service", "Delivery Bill", "BAT"],
        decimals=0,
    )
    .fmt_percent(columns=["MC %"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="HP delivery: what they pay vs. what they cost (weighted mean)")
    .tab_spanner(label="Cost of Service", columns=["Marginal Cost", "MC %", "Residual"])
)
```
:::

Two things jump out from this table. First, **marginal cost is small everywhere** — less than \$211/yr even for ConEd.
The residual dominates cost of service, accounting for 85--98% of it. Second, the gap between bill and cost (the BAT) is
driven almost entirely by how far the bill overshoots, not by how low the cost is.

Let's see this visually. The chart below decomposes each HP customer's delivery bill into three slices: marginal cost,
residual, and the BAT overpayment (the part they shouldn't be paying).

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: fig-bill-decomposition
#| fig-cap: "Each bar represents the mean HP delivery bill, decomposed into marginal cost (what they actually cost the grid), residual share (their equal share of remaining revenue), and BAT overpayment (the cross-subsidy). Marginal cost is barely visible."

bar_rows = []
for row in bill_cost_rows:
    bar_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Component": "Marginal Cost", "Value": row["Marginal Cost"]})
    bar_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Component": "Residual", "Value": row["Residual"]})
    bar_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Component": "BAT (overpayment)", "Value": row["BAT"]})

bar_df = pl.DataFrame(bar_rows)

util_order = (
    bill_cost_df.sort("BAT", descending=True)["Utility"].to_list()
)
comp_order = ["Marginal Cost", "Residual", "BAT (overpayment)"]
comp_colors = {
    "Marginal Cost": SB_COLORS["pistachio"],
    "Residual": SB_COLORS["sky"],
    "BAT (overpayment)": SB_COLORS["carrot"],
}

bar_df = bar_df.with_columns(
    pl.col("Utility").cast(pl.Enum(util_order)),
    pl.col("Component").cast(pl.Enum(comp_order)),
)

(
    ggplot(bar_df, aes(x="Utility", y="Value", fill="Component"))
    + geom_col(width=0.7)
    + scale_fill_manual(values=comp_colors)
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda xs: [f"${x:,.0f}" for x in xs])
    + labs(
        x="",
        y="$/year",
        title="Mean HP delivery bill = marginal cost + residual + overpayment",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(8, 4),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, fontweight="bold"),
        legend_position="top",
    )
)
```
:::

CenHud's bar towers over ConEd's. The carrot slice (BAT) is almost half of CenHud's bill — \$1,737 out of \$3,319.
ConEd's BAT is a much smaller share: \$556 out of \$1,675.

This tells us the question isn't really "why do marginal costs differ?" — they're tiny everywhere. The question is:
**why is the delivery bill so much higher than cost of service for some utilities and not others?**

## The formula: BAT ≈ rate × excess consumption

Per-customer residual allocation gives every customer the same residual amount (HP or not). That amount turns out to
be the average bill minus the average marginal cost. Here's why. The total residual is whatever delivery revenue isn't
explained by marginal costs:

$$
\text{Total Residual} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total MC}
$$

Splitting it equally across $N$ customers:

$$
\text{Residual per customer} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total MC}}{N} = \frac{\sum_i w_i \cdot \text{Bill}_i}{N} - \frac{\sum_i w_i \cdot MC_i}{N} = \overline{\text{Bill}}_u - \overline{\text{MC}}_u
$$

where $\overline{\text{Bill}}_u$ and $\overline{\text{MC}}_u$ are the weighted average delivery bill and marginal cost
across *all* customers in utility $u$. Substituting into the BAT formula:

$$
\text{BAT}_u = \text{Bill}^{HP}_u - \text{MC}^{HP}_u - (\overline{\text{Bill}}_u - \overline{\text{MC}}_u)
$$

$$
= \underbrace{(\text{Bill}^{HP}_u - \overline{\text{Bill}}_u)}_{\text{excess bill}} - \underbrace{(\text{MC}^{HP}_u - \overline{\text{MC}}_u)}_{\text{excess MC}}
$$

Since delivery bills are roughly $\text{Rate} \times \text{kWh}$ (fixed charges are small), the excess bill term is
approximately:

$$
\text{Rate}_u \times (\text{kWh}^{HP}_u - \overline{\text{kWh}}_u)
$$

And since marginal costs are small (< \$211/yr), the excess MC term is tiny. So:

$$
\boxed{\text{BAT}_u \approx \text{Rate}_u \times \Delta\text{kWh}_u}
$$

where $\Delta\text{kWh}_u$ is how much more electricity HP customers consume than the average residential customer in
that utility. The BAT is almost entirely determined by two things: the **delivery rate** and the **excess consumption**
of HP customers over the average.

Let's verify this. We need each utility's delivery rate, HP consumption, and average residential consumption.

## The bill side: rates and consumption

```{python}
#| label: get-delivery-rates

RUN1_DIRS = {
    "cenhud": "20260307_011951_ny_cenhud_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "coned": "20260307_023214_ny_coned_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "nimo": "20260307_020136_ny_nimo_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "nyseg": "20260307_013207_ny_nyseg_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "or": "20260307_011333_ny_or_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "psegli": "20260307_014502_ny_psegli_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
    "rge": "20260307_012508_ny_rge_run1_up00_precalc__flat/",
}

S3_RUNS = "s3://data.sb/switchbox/cairo/outputs/hp_rates/ny"

tariff_info: dict[str, dict] = {}
for util, run_dir in RUN1_DIRS.items():
    path = f"{S3_RUNS}/{util}/{BATCH}/{run_dir}tariff_final_config.json"
    result = subprocess.run(["aws", "s3", "cp", path, "-"], capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode == 0 and result.stdout.strip():
        config = json.loads(result.stdout)
        tariff = config[list(config.keys())[0]]
        fixed = tariff.get("ur_monthly_fixed_charge", 0)
        mat = tariff.get("ur_ec_tou_mat", [[]])
        rate = mat[0][4] if mat and len(mat[0]) > 4 else 0
        tariff_info[util] = {"fixed_monthly": fixed, "rate_per_kwh": rate}
```

Now let's pull average residential consumption from two sources — EIA-861 (real-world utility-reported sales) and
ResStock (the same building simulation we use for BAT) — and compare them to HP consumption backed out from the BAT
data.

::: {.callout-note}
The ResStock average kWh/yr here uses the **original** ResStock 2024.2 load curves (`res_2024_amy2018_2`), not the
Switchbox-corrected loads (`res_2024_amy2018_2_sb`) that reduce miscellaneous appliance consumption. The corrected
release only has hourly load curves on S3 (no pre-aggregated monthly), so we use the uncorrected loads as a proxy. The
corrected loads would be lower — narrowing the gap between ResStock and EIA.
:::

```{python}
#| label: compute-consumption

eia = pl.read_parquet("s3://data.sb/eia/861/electric_utility_stats/")
eia_ny = (
    eia.filter((pl.col("state") == "NY") & (pl.col("year") == 2023))
    .filter(pl.col("utility_code").is_in(list(UTIL_DISPLAY.keys())))
)

eia_kwh: dict[str, float] = {}
eia_customers: dict[str, int] = {}
for row in eia_ny.iter_rows(named=True):
    code = row["utility_code"]
    eia_kwh[code] = row["residential_sales_mwh"] * 1000 / row["residential_customers"]
    eia_customers[code] = int(row["residential_customers"])

RDP_LOCAL = "../../../../rate-design-platform"
PATH_LOAD_CURVES = f"{RDP_LOCAL}/data/nrel/resstock/res_2024_amy2018_2_sb/load_curve_monthly/state=NY/upgrade=00/"
PATH_UTIL_ASSIGN = f"{RDP_LOCAL}/data/nrel/resstock/res_2024_amy2018_2_sb/metadata_utility/state=NY/utility_assignment.parquet"

annual_kwh = (
    pl.scan_parquet(PATH_LOAD_CURVES + "*.parquet")
    .group_by("bldg_id")
    .agg(pl.col("out.electricity.total.energy_consumption").sum().alias("annual_kwh"))
    .collect()
)

util_assign = pl.read_parquet(PATH_UTIL_ASSIGN).select("bldg_id", "sb.electric_utility", "weight")

resstock_kwh_df = annual_kwh.join(util_assign, on="bldg_id")
resstock_kwh: dict[str, float] = {}
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    sub = resstock_kwh_df.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    resstock_kwh[util] = float((sub["annual_kwh"] * sub["weight"]).sum() / sub["weight"].sum())
```

```{python}
#| label: build-driver-table

driver_rows = []
for row in bill_cost_rows:
    util = row["util"]
    rate = tariff_info[util]["rate_per_kwh"]
    fixed = tariff_info[util]["fixed_monthly"]
    hp_bill = row["Delivery Bill"]

    hp_kwh = (hp_bill - fixed * 12) / rate if rate > 0 else 0
    avg_kwh_eia = eia_kwh.get(util, 0)
    avg_kwh_rs = resstock_kwh.get(util, 0)
    delta_eia = hp_kwh - avg_kwh_eia
    delta_rs = hp_kwh - avg_kwh_rs
    predicted_bat = rate * delta_eia

    driver_rows.append({
        "Utility": row["Utility"],
        "util": util,
        "Del. Rate (¢/kWh)": round(rate * 100, 2),
        "HP kWh/yr": round(hp_kwh),
        "Avg (EIA)": round(avg_kwh_eia),
        "Avg (ResStock)": round(avg_kwh_rs),
        "Δ kWh (EIA)": round(delta_eia),
        "Δ kWh (RS)": round(delta_rs),
        "Rate × Δ kWh": round(predicted_bat),
        "Actual BAT": row["BAT"],
        "Residual customers": eia_customers.get(util, 0),
    })

driver_df = pl.DataFrame(driver_rows)
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: driver-table

(
    GT(driver_df.drop("util", "Residual customers"))
    .fmt_number(columns=["Del. Rate (¢/kWh)"], decimals=2)
    .fmt_number(columns=["HP kWh/yr", "Avg (EIA)", "Avg (ResStock)", "Δ kWh (EIA)", "Δ kWh (RS)"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Rate × Δ kWh", "Actual BAT"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="The two drivers of BAT: delivery rate and excess consumption")
    .tab_spanner(label="Avg kWh/yr", columns=["Avg (EIA)", "Avg (ResStock)"])
    .tab_spanner(label="Excess consumption", columns=["Δ kWh (EIA)", "Δ kWh (RS)"])
    .tab_source_note("EIA = EIA-861 (2023) reported sales ÷ customers. ResStock = weighted mean of u0 simulated load curves.")
)
```
:::

The **Rate × Δ kWh** column tracks the actual BAT closely — usually within \$100. The small gap is the excess-MC term we
dropped from the approximation.

Two patterns stand out:

1. **ConEd's HP customers barely consume more than its average residential customer.** The EIA average is 4,478 kWh/yr
   (NYC apartments on tiny AC loads), and the typical HP customer uses \~9,200 kWh/yr — a Δ of about 4,700 kWh. In every
   other utility, the Δ is 8,000--13,000 kWh.

2. **CenHud and PSEG-LI have the worst combination** — both a high delivery rate *and* a large Δ kWh.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: fig-rate-vs-delta
#| fig-cap: "The BAT is the product of delivery rate and excess consumption. ConEd's low Δ kWh is the main reason its BAT is low despite having the highest rate."

plot_df = driver_df.rename({"Δ kWh (EIA)": "delta_kwh", "Del. Rate (¢/kWh)": "del_rate"}).with_columns(
    pl.col("Actual BAT").abs().alias("bat_abs"),
    pl.col("Actual BAT")
    .map_elements(lambda v: f"BAT = ${v:,.0f}", return_dtype=pl.String)
    .alias("bat_label"),
)

(
    ggplot(plot_df, aes(x="delta_kwh", y="del_rate"))
    + geom_point(aes(size="bat_abs"), color=SB_COLORS["midnight"], alpha=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="Utility"), nudge_y=0.5, size=8)
    + labs(
        x="Excess HP consumption over residential avg (kWh/yr)",
        y="Delivery rate (¢/kWh)",
        title="BAT = rate × excess consumption",
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(labels=lambda xs: [f"{x:,.0f}" for x in xs])
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(7, 4), legend_position="none")
)
```
:::

ConEd sits in the bottom-left: its HP customers don't consume much more than its (already low-consuming) average
customer. CenHud is in the upper-right: high rate, high excess. The bubble size (proportional to BAT) tracks the product
of the two axes.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: fig-consumption-comparison
#| fig-cap: "HP customers consume 2--3x the residential average in most utilities. ConEd's HP customers are the exception — they consume only 2x because the building stock is smaller."

cons_plot_rows = []
for row in driver_rows:
    cons_plot_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Type": "Avg (EIA)", "kWh": row["Avg (EIA)"]})
    cons_plot_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Type": "Avg (ResStock)", "kWh": row["Avg (ResStock)"]})
    cons_plot_rows.append({"Utility": row["Utility"], "Type": "HP customer", "kWh": row["HP kWh/yr"]})

cons_plot = pl.DataFrame(cons_plot_rows)

util_order = [r["Utility"] for r in sorted(driver_rows, key=lambda r: r["HP kWh/yr"], reverse=True)]
type_order = ["Avg (EIA)", "Avg (ResStock)", "HP customer"]
type_colors = {"Avg (EIA)": SB_COLORS["sky"], "Avg (ResStock)": SB_COLORS["saffron"], "HP customer": SB_COLORS["midnight"]}

cons_plot = cons_plot.with_columns(
    pl.col("Utility").cast(pl.Enum(util_order)),
    pl.col("Type").cast(pl.Enum(type_order)),
)

(
    ggplot(cons_plot, aes(x="Utility", y="kWh", fill="Type"))
    + geom_col(position=position_dodge(width=0.7), width=0.6)
    + scale_fill_manual(values=type_colors)
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda xs: [f"{x:,.0f}" for x in xs])
    + labs(x="", y="kWh / year", title="HP consumption vs. residential average")
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(6, 3.5), legend_position="top")
)
```
:::

## Why consumption differs: apartments vs. houses

The excess consumption gap is driven by building stock composition. Let's quantify this by looking at what fraction of
each utility's customers live in single-family detached homes vs. multifamily buildings (from the ResStock metadata),
and how that correlates with average electricity consumption.

```{python}
#| label: building-stock-analysis

import numpy as np

util_assign = pl.read_parquet(PATH_UTIL_ASSIGN).select(
    "bldg_id", "sb.electric_utility", "weight", "in.sqft",
    "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
)

bldg_df = resstock_kwh_df.join(
    util_assign.select("bldg_id", "in.sqft", "in.geometry_building_type_recs"),
    on="bldg_id",
)

stock_rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    sub = bldg_df.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    w = sub["weight"]
    total_w = w.sum()
    sfd_w = sub.filter(
        pl.col("in.geometry_building_type_recs") == "Single-Family Detached"
    )["weight"].sum()
    mf5_w = sub.filter(
        pl.col("in.geometry_building_type_recs") == "Multi-Family with 5+ Units"
    )["weight"].sum()
    avg_sqft = float((sub["in.sqft"] * w).sum() / total_w)
    avg_kwh = float((sub["annual_kwh"] * w).sum() / total_w)
    stock_rows.append({
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "% SFD": sfd_w / total_w,
        "% MF 5+": mf5_w / total_w,
        "Avg sqft": round(avg_sqft),
        "Avg kWh/yr": round(avg_kwh),
    })

stock_df = pl.DataFrame(stock_rows)
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: building-stock-table

(
    GT(stock_df)
    .fmt_percent(columns=["% SFD", "% MF 5+"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Avg sqft", "Avg kWh/yr"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .tab_header(title="Building stock composition by utility (ResStock, upgrade 0)")
)
```
:::

ConEd is the outlier: only **12%** single-family detached and **59%** multifamily 5+ units. Every other utility is
60--76% single-family detached. The average ConEd dwelling is 1,178 sqft; upstate dwellings average 1,570--1,830 sqft.

The correlation between % single-family detached and average kWh across the seven utilities is near-perfect:

```{python}
#| label: building-stock-correlations

pct_sfd = np.array([r["% SFD"] for r in stock_rows])
avg_kwh_arr = np.array([r["Avg kWh/yr"] for r in stock_rows])
avg_sqft_arr = np.array([r["Avg sqft"] for r in stock_rows])

r_sfd = np.corrcoef(pct_sfd, avg_kwh_arr)[0, 1]
r_sqft = np.corrcoef(avg_sqft_arr, avg_kwh_arr)[0, 1]

print(f"Corr(% SFD, avg kWh) across utilities: r = {r_sfd:.2f}")
print(f"Corr(avg sqft, avg kWh) across utilities: r = {r_sqft:.2f}")
```

At the utility level, % single-family detached explains **94%** of the variance in average consumption (r² = 0.94).
Building stock composition is almost the entire story.

At the *building* level, the picture is more diffuse — sqft alone explains R² ≈ 0.15 of individual kWh variation, and
adding building type gets to R² ≈ 0.16. There's a lot of within-type variation (insulation quality, occupant behavior,
appliance mix). But the BAT formula operates on *averages*, and at the average level, building stock is deterministic.

## Sensitivity: would full HP adoption change the picture?

The u0 HP sample for some utilities is small (O&R has 26 buildings, CenHud 46). Are the high kWh figures for those
utilities just noise from a thin sample? We can check by looking at run 3+4, which gives *every* building a heat pump
under the same calibrated tariff. This tells us what average HP consumption would look like for the full residential
population, not just the handful of existing HP owners.

```{python}
#| label: load-u2-bills

PATH_BILLS_34 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_3+4/comb_bills_year_target/"
bills_u2 = pl.read_parquet(PATH_BILLS_34, hive_partitioning=True)
annual_u2 = (
    bills_u2
    .group_by("bldg_id", "sb.electric_utility")
    .agg(
        pl.col("elec_delivery_bill").sum().alias("annual_delivery"),
        pl.col("weight").first(),
    )
)
```

```{python}
#| label: build-sensitivity-table

sensitivity_rows = []
for row in driver_rows:
    util = row["util"]
    rate = tariff_info[util]["rate_per_kwh"]
    fixed = tariff_info[util]["fixed_monthly"]

    a = annual_u2.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    bill_u2 = float((a["annual_delivery"] * a["weight"]).sum() / a["weight"].sum())
    kwh_u2 = (bill_u2 - fixed * 12) / rate if rate > 0 else 0

    avg_eia = eia_kwh.get(util, 0)
    delta_u0 = row["HP kWh/yr"] - avg_eia
    delta_u2 = kwh_u2 - avg_eia
    pred_bat_u0 = rate * delta_u0
    pred_bat_u2 = rate * delta_u2

    sensitivity_rows.append({
        "Utility": row["Utility"],
        "N (u0 HP)": driver_df.filter(pl.col("Utility") == row["Utility"])["HP kWh/yr"].len(),
        "kWh (u0 HP)": round(row["HP kWh/yr"]),
        "N (full pop, u2)": a.height,
        "kWh (full pop)": round(kwh_u2),
        "Avg kWh (EIA)": round(avg_eia),
        "Δ kWh (u0)": round(delta_u0),
        "Δ kWh (full pop)": round(delta_u2),
        "Predicted BAT (u0)": round(pred_bat_u0),
        "Predicted BAT (full pop)": round(pred_bat_u2),
    })

sensitivity_df = pl.DataFrame(sensitivity_rows)
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: sensitivity-table

n_u0 = {r["Utility"]: r["N (buildings)"] for r in rows}
sensitivity_display = sensitivity_df.with_columns(
    pl.Series("N (u0 HP)", [n_u0[r["Utility"]] for r in sensitivity_rows]),
)

(
    GT(sensitivity_display.drop("N (full pop, u2)"))
    .fmt_number(columns=["N (u0 HP)"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_number(columns=["kWh (u0 HP)", "kWh (full pop)", "Avg kWh (EIA)", "Δ kWh (u0)", "Δ kWh (full pop)"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Predicted BAT (u0)", "Predicted BAT (full pop)"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="Sensitivity: existing HP sample vs. full-population HP kWh")
    .tab_source_note("u0 = existing HP owners (run 1+2). Full pop = all buildings given HPs (run 3+4, same tariff).")
)
```
:::

The full-population HP kWh is **higher** than the u0 sample in every utility — often by 20--50%. The high kWh figures
for small-sample utilities like O&R and CenHud are not sampling noise; if anything, the existing HP owners *understate*
what average HP consumption would be under widespread adoption. This makes sense: early adopters tend to be in smaller,
more efficient homes. The broader population includes larger, less efficient buildings whose heating loads would drive
up average HP electricity consumption.

The implication is that the BATs we compute from the u0 sample are **conservative**. As HP adoption scales, the average
HP customer will consume more, pushing Δ kWh — and therefore the cross-subsidy — even higher. The structural mismatch
between volumetric delivery rates and actual cost-of-service would get worse, not better, without rate reform.

## Why rates differ: revenue requirements and customer counts

The delivery rate is determined by the revenue requirement divided by total residential kWh. Utilities with high revenue
requirements per kWh of sales will have high rates.

```{python}
#| label: compute-rev-req

RR_DIR = "rate_design/hp_rates/ny/config/rev_requirement"

rr_rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    content = fetch_rdp_file(f"{RR_DIR}/{util}.yaml", ref=RDP_REF)
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(content)
    del_rr = cfg["total_delivery_revenue_requirement"]
    n_cust = eia_customers.get(util, 1)
    rr_rows.append({
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "Del. Rev Req ($M)": del_rr / 1e6,
        "Customers": n_cust,
        "Per-customer ($/yr)": del_rr / n_cust,
        "Del. Rate (¢/kWh)": tariff_info[util]["rate_per_kwh"] * 100,
    })

rr_df = pl.DataFrame(rr_rows)
```

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: rev-req-table

(
    GT(rr_df)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Del. Rev Req ($M)"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Customers"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Per-customer ($/yr)"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Del. Rate (¢/kWh)"], decimals=2)
    .tab_header(title="Delivery revenue requirement per customer")
)
```
:::

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: fig-rev-req-per-customer
#| fig-cap: "Per-customer delivery revenue requirement varies modestly across utilities — less than the 3x variation in BAT. The rate difference is driven more by how much each customer consumes than by how much infrastructure each customer costs."

rr_plot = rr_df.rename({"Per-customer ($/yr)": "per_cust"}).with_columns(
    pl.col("Utility").cast(pl.Enum([r["Utility"] for r in rr_rows])),
    pl.col("per_cust")
    .map_elements(lambda v: f"${v:,.0f}", return_dtype=pl.String)
    .alias("label"),
)

(
    ggplot(rr_plot, aes(x="Utility", y="per_cust"))
    + geom_col(width=0.6, fill=SB_COLORS["sky"])
    + geom_text(aes(label="label"), nudge_y=30, size=8)
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda xs: [f"${x:,.0f}" for x in xs])
    + labs(x="", y="$/customer/year", title="Delivery revenue requirement per customer")
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(8, 4))
)
```
:::

CenHud and ConEd both have high delivery rates (\~14--15 ¢/kWh), but for different reasons. ConEd's revenue requirement
is enormous in absolute terms — it serves millions of customers in the most expensive infrastructure environment in
the state — but spread over those customers, the *per-customer* delivery cost is modest. CenHud is much smaller and its
per-customer infrastructure costs are high.

RG&E has the lowest delivery rate (7.2 ¢/kWh), which means its excess-consumption penalty is smaller per kWh. But its Δ
kWh is large enough that the BAT still comes out to \$912/yr.

## The cost side: why marginal costs barely matter

Given the formula $\text{BAT} = \text{Rate} \times \Delta\text{kWh} - \Delta\text{MC}$, one might expect the marginal
cost difference to provide a meaningful offset. It doesn't.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: mc-comparison

mc_rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    hp_sub = hp.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    all_sub = bat.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    mc_hp = _wmean(hp_sub, "economic_burden_delivery")
    mc_all = _wmean(all_sub, "economic_burden_delivery")
    mc_rows.append({
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "MC (HP)": round(mc_hp),
        "MC (all)": round(mc_all),
        "Δ MC": round(mc_hp - mc_all),
    })

(
    GT(pl.DataFrame(mc_rows))
    .fmt_currency(columns=["MC (HP)", "MC (all)", "Δ MC"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="Marginal delivery cost: HP vs. all customers")
)
```
:::

The excess MC (Δ MC) ranges from \$14 to \$55 across utilities. Compare this to the excess bill (Rate × Δ kWh), which
ranges from \$500 to \$1,800. **Marginal cost differences explain less than 5% of the BAT variation.** Delivery marginal
costs in 2025 are simply too small — relative to delivery bills — to meaningfully offset the cross-subsidy.

The geographic pattern in MC is real (NYC-area utilities face higher bulk transmission costs from Cambium than upstate
utilities) but it barely registers in the BAT arithmetic.

## Supply-side cross-subsidy: a secondary effect

The analysis above focuses on delivery, which is where the cross-subsidy is largest. But HP customers also experience a
supply-side BAT. Let's check whether supply compounds or offsets the delivery effect.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| label: supply-vs-delivery

sd_rows = []
for util in UTIL_ORDER_BY_BAT:
    sub = hp.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    sd_rows.append({
        "Utility": UTIL_DISPLAY.get(util, util),
        "Delivery BAT": round(_wmean(sub, "BAT_percustomer_delivery")),
        "Supply BAT": round(_wmean(sub, "BAT_percustomer_supply")),
        "Total BAT": round(_wmean(sub, "BAT_percustomer_total")),
    })

(
    GT(pl.DataFrame(sd_rows))
    .fmt_currency(columns=["Delivery BAT", "Supply BAT", "Total BAT"], decimals=0)
    .tab_header(title="Delivery vs. supply BAT for HP customers")
)
```
:::

Supply adds \$148--\$331/yr for most utilities. ConEd is the exception: its supply BAT is essentially zero, meaning
ConEd's supply bill almost exactly matches supply cost-of-service. The delivery cross-subsidy is the dominant effect.

## Summary

The 3x variation in delivery overpayment across utilities is structural, not an artifact. It follows directly from a
simple formula:

$$
\text{BAT} \approx \text{Delivery Rate} \times (\text{kWh}_{HP} - \overline{\text{kWh}})
$$

- **ConEd** has the highest delivery rate but the **lowest BAT**, because its HP customers are NYC apartments that
  don't consume much more than the average ConEd customer ($\Delta$kWh ≈ 4,700). The denominator of the cross-subsidy
  is small.

- **CenHud** has the **highest BAT** because it combines a high delivery rate (14.5 ¢/kWh) with a large excess
  consumption ($\Delta$kWh ≈ 13,300). Single-family homes with heat pumps consume far more than CenHud's residential
  average.

- **Marginal costs don't meaningfully differentiate utilities.** The MC offset is less than 5% of the BAT in every
  utility. The cross-subsidy is almost entirely a rate-times-volume story.

This has a direct policy implication: **a fair seasonal rate must be calibrated per utility**, because the magnitude of
the cross-subsidy depends on utility-specific rate levels and building stock composition. A single statewide correction
would overcorrect for ConEd and undercorrect for CenHud.
